title: Norbert Wiener and Why Engineering Got Interested in Biology

lines.text: Section eight. Norbert Wiener and the moment engineering got interested in biology.
Two books, both published in 1948, set the stage for nearly everything we now call systems thinking.
The first was Norbert Wiener's book Cybernetics — subtitled Or Control and Communication in the Animal and the Machine.
The second was Claude Shannon's mathematical theory of communication, which laid the foundations of information theory.

Together, these two works did something unusual: they argued that the same mathematical machinery should be applied to living systems, electronic systems, mechanical systems, economic systems, and informational systems alike.

Wiener, in particular, took the view that any system in which signals flow and feedback exists could be described in the same formal language — whether the signals carry chemical messengers, electrical voltages, or social information.
That perspective, generalised and extended over the subsequent decades, became what we now call systems theory.
The textbook explicitly traces synthetic biology's analytical toolkit back to Wiener's intervention.

If you ever wonder why engineers feel so at home with the language of feedback, transfer functions, gain, stability, and noise when talking about biology, this is the reason: the conceptual move that says "biology is a system, and the mathematics of systems is the same regardless of the substrate" was Wiener's gift to the field.
Let me unpack what systems theory actually is.

Systems theory is concerned with general principles for analysing systems — properties of inputs, outputs, internal states, dynamics, and feedback — that apply regardless of what the system is physically made of.
A system, in this framework, is anything with inputs, outputs, and an internal mapping that converts one to the other.
An aircraft autopilot is a system: the input is the desired heading, the output is the actual heading, and the internal mapping involves sensors, actuators, and a controller.
An economy is a system: the inputs are interest rates and government policy, the outputs are GDP and employment, the internal mapping is whatever you believe the economy's structure to be.

A bacterial chemotaxis pathway is a system: the input is a gradient of attractant or repellent, the output is the direction of motion, the internal mapping is the signalling cascade between receptor and flagellar motor.
Each of these is a system in exactly the same formal sense — the same vocabulary of state, transfer function, feedback, and dynamics applies.

What systems theory does is develop general theorems about what kinds of behaviours systems can produce, what kinds of feedback structures are stable, what kinds of inputs can be tracked, what kinds of disturbances can be rejected.
It turns out — and this is genuinely remarkable — that the same theorems hold whether the system is a heart, a thermostat, an aircraft, or a gene circuit.
This is why synthetic biology can co-opt the engineering vocabulary so cleanly.
Coupled with systems theory is signal theory — also called signal processing.
Signal theory is the body of mathematics that deals with how signals — time-varying quantities — are represented, transformed, and analysed.
It originated in telecommunications, was elaborated in radar and audio engineering, and now permeates every quantitative field including modern biology.
The two together, systems theory and signal theory, are the technical backbone of how engineers think about dynamics.
And both, the textbook reminds us, were largely formalised by Wiener and Shannon in 1948 and have been developed by countless researchers since.

Why are we taking this historical detour in a synthetic biology textbook? Because the authors want you to recognise that the analytical methods we are about to apply to gene circuits — Laplace transforms, frequency-domain analysis, block diagrams, control theory — are not biology-specific tricks.
They are general engineering tools, and the reason they work on biology is precisely Wiener's argument that biology is a system.

So when, in the next section, we start talking about Fourier transforms of biological waveforms, treat this not as a strange biology-specific manoeuvre but as engineering business as usual, applied to a biological substrate.
That mental move is one of the most important things this chapter is trying to teach you.
